segments.text: Good morning.
Thank you so much for, uh, taking the time to join us, um,
this morning for this briefing, uh,
into your report, a new chapter.
How well are the changes to English
and maths going really appreciate, uh,
the time you've got with us this morning.
We've got 45 minutes, including, um, time
for a lot of questions, I imagine.
Um, so if we could kick off
with giving you the opportunity just to introduce yourselves
to everyone and, uh, maybe some opening remarks,
and then we'll, we'll go to questions.
Okay. Well, I'll begin. So, uh,
for the last two days, okay.
I'll share with my colleague Ru Kiera.
Um, I'm the Deputy Chief Executive, uh, education Review
Evaluation policy.
Uh, Ana Coto.
My name is Rebecca Di,
and I'm senior manager in at the EDUC office.
And our last colleague, we'll introduce here, Katie Welsh,
uh, who is supporting us in terms of
where your questions might potentially go.
Does Katie want to come up to the table?
I'm happy for her to sit on
that last chair there if you'd like.
Hay. Sorry. I understood Heidi. Thanks, Shannon.
Okay, well, can I, uh, begin by thanking the committee
for inviting us, uh, here this morning, uh, to share
with you our insights from our look into an exploration
of the implementation of mathematics in English.
Uh, the Arrow review studies into the
implementation of curriculum have been kind of a standard,
uh, standard piece of our work program over many years.
Uh, but this program was specifically requested
of the minister in terms of the, the provisions
that sit in the, the act.
Uh, the study looks at quite ly,
uh, the mathematics, uh,
and English rollouts at your years N to eight in,
in, uh, in our primary schools.
And we undertook the study during, uh, term four
of last year and term one and two of this year.
And, uh, in terms of Haley's work, uh,
it has been supplemented by findings from the latter part
of, of last year into the, the phonics,
the first phonics results.
Uh, this evaluation is not an
evaluation of the curriculum content,
so it's not a dissection of, of what's in there,
what makes up the curriculum,
and it's equally not a dissection of the process
to deliver and, and, uh, develop the curriculum material.
It is solely limited to how well
is it being implemented.
Uh, and,
and this is part of a multi-year study, so we'll continue
to go back and look at, uh, in subsequent years
how it's continuing to, to roll out and,
and embed and be sustained.
Uh, our questions, uh,
included what's been taught, uh, in the,
in the refreshed learning areas.
So which aspects of the curriculum are,
are being prioritized, uh,
or is it across the board, uh,
the early impacts, most particularly that we are able
to glean in terms of student outcomes?
And lastly, what's changed for parents and whanau?
Uh, as I've said,
the reforms occur in a context, uh, where,
uh, aero has been very critical of key trends
and outcomes across a ROA in literacy and numeracy.
Uh, we have been concerned both at the proportion
of students who are significantly behind
in curriculum levels,
but as importantly, the huge equity gaps
that we see in our system.
And lastly, uh, based on the OECD studies
of maths and reading and, and,
and so on, the, uh, stagnation in some of those trends
or decline that we've seen in, in some of those patterns.
Um, so that, that provides a, a significant context
along with, uh, our look into previous rollouts
of curriculum reform, such as, uh,
more recently the digital technologies curriculum rollout,
uh, to Fari, uh, to name, name a couple of of those.
Uh, I also think,
uh, as a context we have
since 2007,
and at that time, the introduction
of the New Zealand curriculum, uh, also looked at and,
and surfaced a range of concerns, uh, with
what was going on in schools
and the capacity of teachers to actually give effect to,
to their curriculum.
So they all provide a context to, to the concerns
that we are, uh, dealing with, uh, in this study.
And Ruth will go through those findings.
Uh, we have, uh, uncovered very different results to,
to what we've seen historically, uh, as, as an evaluator
and a root review officer.
And, um, at this point, I'd like to commend the ministry
because I think they have done a superb job
in the rollout of this.
Uh, they have taken on feedback

uh, a shared in quite, uh, graphic detail, uh,
some of our observations from previous implementations.
Uh, but most particularly, I want
to acknowledge the teachers, uh, toki across
AA who have really, uh, gripped this up, uh,
and who were the subject of our, our work.

Thank you. I'm happy to go to questions then.

wanna check if, uh, welcome everyone.
If Ruth, if you want to say anything
or are you happy to first go, go to
Questions? Um, sure. I mean, the, the
main thing is thank you so much

Um, so it's definitely deliberately early in implementation

and adjust as we go.
So it is in that context.
So thank you for giving us the opportunity.

Happy to have a, some highlights of the findings. Mm-hmm.

But fantastic. So just
some quick highlights, conscious of your time.
Um, very encouraging signs of improved progress in English
and maths, which against the backdrop Nick et out
is really encouraging.
So half of teachers reported achievement improving
in English and maths.
Three quarters of parents agree
that their child's making quicker progress.
We've seen rapid progress,
particularly in the phonics tests.
Um, in term one, only the third
were at where they needed to be.
And by term three it was over half.
We've reduced the number of students behind in
that phonics test in term one over half needed support in
term three, only a third.
And we've doubled the number
of students exceeding expectations.
Now, these are early days,
but they are very encouraging findings.
It has happened because schools have changed
what they teach, how they teach,
and how much they teach English and maths.
So on what nearly all schools are
implementing new curriculum.
And very positively, nine of out of 10 in English
and eight out of 10 in maths are teaching all the elements.
Now, this early on, that is a real testament
to the work that schools have done.
Um, secondly on the, how eight out
of 10 teachers have changed
how they teach English and maths.
They report change in their practice.
One of the biggest shifts is in more explicit teaching.
Two thirds of teachers, for example,
said on structured literacy.
Their teachers practice have changed a
lot in that short time.
That is a very big shift on the how much a third
of schools have increased the time they spend teaching,
reading, writing, and maths.
And one of the most encouraging findings for me was
that in some of our lower socioeconomic communities,
that had been the biggest increase in the time spent in
teaching maths with half saying they've increased the time
they're teaching maths.
Um, this has happened. We found
because the package of support the ministry put in
was highly impactful.
So eight out of 10 teachers have accessed what we call PLD,
professional learning and development in both English
and three quarters in maths.
They found it useful,
more useful than all the other PLD we've been studying at
Hero and we did this whole study early this year about
professional learning development.
So for example, on structured literacy, seven in 10 teachers
use it every day.
The training they did in terms of impact, again, hats off
to the ministry teachers
who access the maths resources worth four times more likely
to change their practice.
We have a very big data set
so we can do these kind of regressions.
Mm-hmm. Teachers who act
as the English guidance were over three times more likely
to change their practice.
And lastly, parents.
So parents know what to do and are helping their children.
So nine outta 10 parents know what to do
and are helping them at home.
Parents with different backgrounds are equally helping
and equally knowing what to do.
And that is unusual.
We normally see a bit more
of a difference depending on backgrounds education,
but parents in this one all equally know what to do
and are supporting their students at home.
And lastly, as Nick said, teachers
and leaders, nine outta 10 leaders say they know
what to do to implement it.
Nine outta 10, appointed someone to lead it.
Uh, eight outta 10 have a plan.
Now, obviously we did this review
to find out if there's anything we could strengthen.
And later in questions we can come on
to some areas which we say we do need to do a bit more.
Um, but for early days,
this is the most encouraging report we have seen on an
implementation this early on.
And it's a testament to the work of our teachers
and leaders across the country.
Fabulous. Thank you. Ruth. Um,
I think it would be com everyone would be comfortable
to hear from you on those, uh, suggested areas
of improvement before we get a questions
or do you wanna Yeah, sure. Go for
It. Yeah, go for it. Yeah.
We've got some, so three areas.
So, and that's why we did it early
to see what can we adjust?
And I know the ministry's on to these. So three things.
One, maths, as you know, maths has been a bit
of a problem, uh, in terms of teaching.
So we're not seeing the expected shift in the complexity
of maths as we go from year zero, right up the years
to up to year seven and eight.
So three quarters of teachers teach number number's,
great number's important,
but only a quarter are teaching probability a lot.
Mm-hmm. And that's not enough. This is due to three things.
One, the shift in number is big.
Um, second, New Zealand students have always struggled
with some of those basic numbers, but
thirdly, teachers lack confidence.
Some teachers in the more complex maths true.
And a previous study we did on new teachers,
you may remember a quarter primary school teachers said
that they were unprepared to teach maths.
So we are saying more support is needed to make sure
that these early encouraging signs isn't just the basics,
but as the kids are progressing, the more complex maths.
So that's area one. There's just three areas.
Uh, the second area is smaller rural schools.
So they're doing a great job.
They're making equal amounts of change,
but they're finding it harder.
Mm-hmm. So the fewer of them have got plans in place.
Um, fewer have got someone to lead it,
and they struggle to access resources and guidance.
So we've said we need a bespoke kind of program
because if you're the only, uh,
lead only teacher in the school, it's really hard.
So we've said something different needs to be put in place
for small and rural schools.
And lastly, thirdly, it's great this early
that teachers have all got the basics
and they've worked really hard on it.
But now teachers are saying to us
that they'd like more support about catching kids
up and extending them.
And that's the next, so teachers are not always sure yet how
to adapt, and that's because it's new.
So they're not quite sure what do I teach like this
and what can I change?
And particularly if they've got classes,
which we often find in New Zealand,
particularly in smaller communities
where they've thought many children
of different levels in the class
or different ages, that's more challenging to teach.
So we said again, so those are the three areas
where we are calling for change in implementation.
Um, small schools, let's do something bespoke,
more support on complicated maths.
And then lastly, let's make sure that teachers know how
to catch kids up and extend them.
And that's why we did the evaluation so early is so
that we can learn this as we go and adjust.
Fantastic. Lawrence Cook. Thank you. Uh, chair.

Three lessons. One,

It's signaled and sequenced

So it can work.

source_metadata.title: 2025/11/19 - EW
source_metadata.description:
Wednesday 19 November 2025 | Education and Workforce Committee

Briefing on the report of the Education Review Office: A new chapter, How well are the changes to English and maths going?

Hearing of Evidence:

Education Review Office
   Nicholas Pole, Chief Executive and Chief Review Officer
   Ruth Shinoda, Deputy Chief Executive, Evaluation and Policy
   Rebecca Bjarnesen, Senior Manager, Evaluation and Policy

Ministry of Education
   Hayley Welch, Acting General Manager, New Zealand Curriculum and Te Whāriki, Te Poutāhū (Curriculum Centre)
